title: Trees
description: Binary trees are among the most tested interview topics — master the TreeNode struct, DFS traversals (inorder/preorder/postorder), BFS with a queue, and recursive depth calculation.

sections.title: TreeNode struct and construction
sections.explanation: Most C++ interview problems use a simple **TreeNode struct** with `val`, `left`, and `right` pointers. Initialize with `new TreeNode(val)` and link children manually. Always use `nullptr` for null children (not NULL). Interviewers expect you to define this struct from memory.

exercise.prompt: Define a TreeNode struct. Build a tree with root=10, left child=5, right child=15. Print the root value, left child value, and right child value.

validation.message: Tree should have root=10, left=5, right=15
Pass the values 10, 5, 15 to the TreeNode constructor

exercise.hint: Pass 10, 5, and 15 as arguments to new TreeNode().

sections.title: DFS traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder)
sections.explanation: **Inorder** (left, root, right) produces sorted output for BSTs and is the most commonly tested. **Preorder** (root, left, right) is used for serialization. **Postorder** (left, right, root) is used for deletion and bottom-up computation. All three use the same recursive pattern with a base case of `if (!node) return;`.

exercise.prompt: Implement inorder DFS traversal. Build a tree: root=4, left=2, right=6. Run the traversal and print the result space-separated.

validation.message: Inorder traversal of this BST produces sorted order: 2 4 6
Recurse left, push node->val, then recurse right for inorder

exercise.hint: Inorder: recurse on node->left, push node->val, recurse on node->right.

sections.title: BFS level-order traversal
sections.explanation: Level-order traversal uses a **queue<TreeNode*>** to process nodes breadth-first. To group nodes by level, iterate `queue.size()` times per level before moving to the next. This pattern appears in problems like zigzag traversal, right-side view, and minimum depth.

exercise.prompt: Implement BFS level-order traversal. Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (children 4,5), right=3. Print each level on its own line, values space-separated.

validation.message: Use queue front/pop for FIFO and push children for next level

exercise.hint: q.empty() for loop condition. q.size() to count current level. q.front() to read, q.pop() to remove.

sections.title: Tree depth (recursive)
sections.explanation: Maximum depth is the number of nodes on the longest root-to-leaf path. The classic recursive formula: `1 + max(depth(left), depth(right))` with base case 0 for nullptr. This O(n) pattern is a building block for balanced-tree checks, diameter calculations, and subtree problems.

exercise.prompt: Implement a recursive maxDepth function. Build a tree: root=1, left=2 (left child=4, which has left child=8), right=3. Print the max depth.

validation.message: Base case returns 0, recurse on left and right, take the max + 1

exercise.hint: Base case: return 0 for nullptr. Recursive: 1 + max(maxDepth(node->left), maxDepth(node->right)).
